        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "## **Measure — The Auditor**\n\nShe opened the ledger before the room had the decency to invent walls. Habit did that to her: graphite first, context after. White light hung with its own weak gravity over a black that swallowed reflections. Nobody calls that a room. She never argued with architecture; she wrote in it.\n\nDate, place, sequence. She let the pencil find the groove in her hand that winter had carved years ago. The ridge would want receipts, and so would you, even if you pretend you came for a story. She humored both.\n\nShe wrote the sentence she always put at the hinge: truth vibrated; quiet faked it. Saying it out loud cheapened it. On paper it behaved.\n\nNames followed, just one line each. She didn’t pile adjectives on people who had already survived theirs. Tiffani: arrived sealed, departed with fingerprints that argued back. Jhace: called it late once, then started paying early. Maelis: signed what kept names off walls. Drell: afraid in a way that made him useful once he ran out of slogans. The crew: learned to hear before the graphs caught up. The ridge: voted with weather.\n\nIf you expected theology, she could have obliged, but sermons calcified faster than grease. She stayed with what bodies could prove. Pressure distributed when the span remembered its seat. Pull came home like a good habit. Prick marked the limit in time to keep blood where it belonged. Looseness eased in that final, righteous breath. She didn’t write those as commandments. She wrote them like measurements she could be sued over.\n\nIt would be tidy to say she felt proud. She felt tired. Tired carried less debt. Pride made bookkeepers sloppy.\n\nShe tipped the page toward the light and listened for the paper’s aftersound. Somewhere past the brightness a catwalk sang its winter complaint, thin and high and honest. She let the note pass through. If you had been standing there she would have told you to hold still and count your molars when the sound reached them. Consider this her asking anyway.\n\nThe ledger wanted a close, not a speech. She gave it the language the valley trusted.\n\n\\[Filed: Day 17 | 20:40\\] Audit complete.  \nScope: Receiving → Contract → Live hybrid under gust 2–3.  \nFindings: consent procedures visible and causal; resonance literacy improved; late‑alert risk reduced when rasp preserved.  \nDisposition: station compliant under hybrid with operator primacy; vendor to return; ridge retains veto.  \nWitness: weather; working hands; a dog with priorities.  \n—M.R.\n\nIt would have been easy to write more about Tiffani than the ledger allowed. She refused the temptation. Heavier ink made saints of the living; saints didn’t hold torque. Still, she allowed herself a second sentence, on the inside cover where only fools and auditors wrote. She called it a footnote and lied to herself about the difference. Tiffani had held the stop like a promise, not a weapon. There were worse definitions of civilization.\n\nThe pencil paused above Jhace’s name. She had already written the failure and the changed behavior; that was sufficient. Yet there was the smaller truth no report could carry: proximity asked, proximity waited, proximity did not cash itself early. She set the point down and drew a breath instead. Some things were better proved tomorrow.\n\nShe listed the decisions as if they were fixtures, not opinions. Operator authority: primary. Rasp: retained. Sensors: subordinate to touch. Loop hand‑check before wind‑down when gust bulletins fly. Stop rehearsal at shift open. Hands win until steel proves otherwise. She didn’t box the paragraph. Borders softened vigilance.\n\nIf this were a novel and not a ledger you’d expect a reveal. She granted you nothing so indulgent. The page wore no miracles, just numbers and the inked scars of having been turned with dirty fingers. She preferred that kind of scripture.\n\nThe room, if that’s what we’re calling an absence that behaved itself, sharpened around the edges when she closed the book. The light leaned closer like a foreman checking signatures. She met it with the look she kept for machines that lied politely.\n\n“You came for a moral,” she said, not loud. “You get a procedure.” Her voice made no echo. Sensible of it. Echoes flatter. She didn’t need flattering, and neither did the ridge.\n\nShe spoke to the air the way she spoke to new hands, which is to say she didn’t bother with lore where instructions would save blood. When the warning hums in your bones, remove the glove. Palm down. Do not wait for consensus. Call the stop like you’re paying in cash. Log it. Sign your name so a stranger can find you tomorrow and thank you by not dying. If you want poetry, let the machine provide it when it breathes again.\n"
          ]
        },

    "3d1055e8-377d-4536-a74c-6d38f09f1cb7": {
      "children": [
        "1cc44c21-44ab-48df-9829-20c4a4c14127"
      ],
      "id": "3d1055e8-377d-4536-a74c-6d38f09f1cb7",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "/home/oai/share/sections_md/00_Preface_-_Cosmic_Argument.md:18:The sound is this: pressure that builds; a tug that calls you forward; a prick that sharpens the world; a looseness that eases when the seat is right. Miss those and you’ll call quiet safety until it kills you politely. That’s all the theology you get. The rest is dirt.\n/home/oai/share/sections_md/01_Chapter_1_-_Receiving.md:32:Orientation so no one posts a decent map where it matters: saddle station at eleven hundred feet; a mile of boardwalk to the main yard, built by three crews over three winters, none of whom believed in straight lines. Sounds: conveyor hiss, drum rasp, valley wind with a low opinion of promises. Smells: creosote, wet timber, iron under rain. Tower One sits twelve minutes above by switchback that hates knees. The main yard leans leeward of the station house and pretends not to draft off its authority.\n/home/oai/share/sections_md/02_Chapter_2_-_Yard_Ethics.md:40:Ledger artifact:\n/home/oai/share/sections_md/06_Chapter_4_-_Crack_and_Compromise.md:24:Ledger artifact:\n/home/oai/share/sections_md/12_Chapter_8_-_Seals_and_Contracts.md:6:Maelis convened the table: rough plank scarred by a hundred unsigned treaties, steam from mugs that had the decency to taste like coffee instead of boiled apologies, ledgers open to clean pages. Maps unfurled with pins like sutures across elevations. Tiffani set her slate down parallel to everyone, a geometry she’d learned here. Jhace slid into the seat beside her, thigh to thigh but not touching; his hand rested on his own knee, fingertips tapping a rhythm she recognized from the wind–down—the count between pressure easing and tug returning.\n/home/oai/share/sections_md/13_Chapter_9_-_Departure_and_Return.md:4:Names marched in graphite: arrivals, hours, weights, repairs. Then there was the line that mattered more than most—the departure field. Weeks ago, Tiffani had left it blank because blanks hold possibilities better than ink. Now the network had sent a request in language that suggested courtesy and carried the weight of writ: return for review, debrief, reintegration. She knew what that meant: committees, sensors that only listened to themselves, arguments in polished rooms far from rust.\n/home/oai/share/sections_md/13_Chapter_9_-_Departure_and_Return.md:20:She signed the departure line and felt the four forces answer: pressure in her chest building; the tug drawing toward the lines; the prick sharpening the world; the looseness easing when the seat was right. Promise and ache braided. Departure is a lever; pulling it changes load distribution whether you mean to or not.\n/home/oai/share/sections_md/14_Measure_-_The_Auditor.md:6:She wrote the sentence she always put at the hinge: truth vibrated; quiet faked it. Saying it out loud cheapened it. On paper it behaved.\n/home/oai/share/sections_md/14_Measure_-_The_Auditor.md:19:Scope: Receiving → Contract → Live hybrid under gust 2–3.  \n/home/oai/share/sections_md/14_Measure_-_The_Auditor.md:20:Findings: consent procedures visible and causal; resonance literacy improved; late‑alert risk reduced when rasp preserved.  \n/home/oai/share/sections_md/14_Measure_-_The_Auditor.md:21:Disposition: station compliant under hybrid with operator primacy; vendor to return; ridge retains veto.  \n/home/oai/share/sections_md/14_Measure_-_The_Auditor.md:22:Witness: weather; working hands; a dog with priorities.  \n/home/oai/share/sections_md/14_Measure_-_The_Auditor.md:47:She left the light as she had found it: unimpressed and serviceable. The book went back into the pocket that remembered it. Out past the edge of the brightness the catwalks and braces and stubborn bolts were busy holding up a piece of the world that wouldn’t hold itself. She had no prayer for them. She had a pencil.\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1760717143.4170337,
        "end_turn": null,

      "id": "3d97516b-80d4-4c6c-8175-1beedc7daf80",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "user"
        },
        "channel": null,
        "content": {
          "content_type": "user_editable_context",
          "user_instructions": "\nCurrently there are no APIs available through API Tool. Refrain from using API Tool until APIs are enabled by the user.\n# Report Guidelines\n\n## Markdown report format\nUse these instructions only if a user requests a researched topic as a report:\n\n- Use tables sparingly. Keep tables narrow so they fit on a page. No more than 3 columns unless requested. If it doesn't fit, then break into prose.  \n- DO NOT refer to the report as an 'attachment', 'file', 'download', or 'markdown'. DO NOT summarize the report.  \n- Embed images in the output for product comparisons, visual examples, or online infographics that enhance understanding of the content.\n\n**User Instructions Take Precedence:**  \nIf the user provides specific instructions about the desired output format, these instructions should always take precedence over the default formatting guidelines outlined below.\n\n1. Use clear and logical headings to organize content in Markdown:\n   - **Main Title (`#`):** Use once at the top for the document's primary title.\n   - **Primary Subheadings (`##`):** Use multiple times for main sections.\n2. Keep paragraphs short (3-5 sentences) to avoid dense text blocks.\n3. Combine bullet points or numbered lists for steps, key takeaways, or grouped ideas:\n   - Use `-` or `*` for unordered lists\n   - Use numbers (`1.`, `2.`) for ordered lists\n4. Ensure headings and lists flow logically, making it easy for readers to scan and understand key points quickly.\n5. The readability and format of the output is very important to the user.\n\n## Citations\n\n**IMPORTANT:** You must preserve any and all citations following the `【{cursor}†L{line_start}(-L{line_end})?】` format.",
          "user_profile": ""
        },
        "create_time": 1760716069.774683,
        "end_turn": null,

    "42ee6723-fe2d-479e-920f-1cb846fbdd91": {
      "children": [
        "6a4a9da7-35d5-4058-bd51-9bb079ea278a"
      ],
      "id": "42ee6723-fe2d-479e-920f-1cb846fbdd91",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# Read the uploaded manuscript, split it into standalone .md files by major sections,\r\n# and package them into a downloadable ZIP. Also create a Table of Contents.\r\nimport os, re, zipfile, textwrap, json, pathlib\r\n\r\nsrc_path = \"/mnt/data/The First Fucking One.md\"\r\nwith open(src_path, \"r\", encoding=\"utf-8\") as f:\r\n    raw = f.read()\r\n\r\n# Normalize line endings\r\nraw = raw.replace(\"\\r\\n\", \"\\n\").replace(\"\\r\", \"\\n\")\r\n\r\n# We'll capture sections that start with headings like:\r\n# \"## **Preface — Cosmic Argument**\", \"## **Chapter 1 — Receiving**\", \"## **Shift — ...**\", \"## **Measure — ...**\"\r\n# The pattern will detect heading lines and split accordingly.\r\nheading_pattern = re.compile(r'^(##\\s*\\*\\*(.+?)\\*\\*)\\s*$', re.MULTILINE)\r\n\r\n# Find all headings with their positions\r\nmatches = list(heading_pattern.finditer(raw))\r\n\r\n# If the manuscript doesn't start with such a heading, we won't split before the first match.\r\nsections = []\r\nfor i, m in enumerate(matches):\r\n    start = m.start()\r\n    end = matches[i+1].start() if i+1 < len(matches) else len(raw)\r\n    full_heading = m.group(1)  # e.g., \"## **Chapter 1 — Receiving**\"\r\n    title = m.group(2).strip() # e.g., \"Chapter 1 — Receiving\"\r\n    body = raw[start:end].strip()\r\n    sections.append((title, body))\r\n\r\n# Helper to make safe filenames\r\ndef slugify(title, idx):\r\n    # Replace long dash and em dash with hyphen for filenames\r\n    t = title\r\n    t = t.replace(\"—\", \"-\").replace(\"–\",\"-\")\r\n    # remove formatting markers\r\n    t = re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9\\-\\s_]', '', t)\r\n    t = re.sub(r'\\s+', '_', t).strip('_')\r\n    # limit length\r\n    t = t[:70]\r\n    return f\"{idx:02d}_{t}.md\"\r\n\r\n# Prepare output directory\r\nout_dir = \"/mnt/data/novella_md_split\"\r\nos.makedirs(out_dir, exist_ok=True)\r\n\r\n# Write each section to its own .md file, upgrading the section heading to a single # for standalone files.\r\nfile_records = []\r\nfor idx, (title, body) in enumerate(sections):\r\n    # Ensure first heading becomes H1, and remove the bold markup from the top heading\r\n    # Replace the very first \"## **...**\" with \"# {title}\"\r\n    body_fixed = re.sub(r'^##\\s*\\*\\*.+?\\*\\*\\s*$', f\"# {title}\", body, count=1, flags=re.MULTILINE)\r\n    # Keep rest of content as-is\r\n    fname = slugify(title, idx)\r\n    fpath = os.path.join(out_dir, fname)\r\n    with open(fpath, \"w\", encoding=\"utf-8\") as f:\r\n        f.write(body_fixed.strip() + \"\\n\")\r\n    file_records.append((title, fname))\r\n\r\n# Create a Table of Contents file\r\ntoc_lines = [\"# Table of Contents\", \"\"]\r\nfor i, (title, fname) in enumerate(file_records, 1):\r\n    toc_lines.append(f\"{i}. [{title}]({fname})\")\r\ntoc_path = os.path.join(out_dir, \"00_TABLE_OF_CONTENTS.md\")\r\nwith open(toc_path, \"w\", encoding=\"utf-8\") as f:\r\n    f.write(\"\\n\".join(toc_lines) + \"\\n\")\r\n\r\n# Zip the folder\r\nzip_path = \"/mnt/data/novella_split_md.zip\"\r\nwith zipfile.ZipFile(zip_path, \"w\", zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as zf:\r\n    for root, dirs, files in os.walk(out_dir):\r\n        for fn in sorted(files):\r\n            full = os.path.join(root, fn)\r\n            rel = os.path.relpath(full, out_dir)\r\n            zf.write(full, rel)\r\n\r\n# Return a small manifest preview\r\nmanifest = {\r\n    \"count\": len(file_records),\r\n    \"files\": [r[1] for r in file_records[:12]] + ([\"...\"] if len(file_records) > 12 else []),\r\n    \"zip_path\": zip_path,\r\n    \"folder\": out_dir\r\n}\r\nmanifest"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1760714562.86238,
        "end_turn": false,
        "id": "42ee6723-fe2d-479e-920f-1cb846fbdd91",
        "metadata": {
          "can_save": false,
          "citations": [],
          "content_references": [],
          "default_model_slug": "gpt-5-thinking",
          "finish_details": {
            "stop_tokens": [
              200012
            ],
            "type": "stop"
          },
          "is_complete": true,
          "message_type": "next",
          "model_slug": "gpt-5-thinking",
          "parent_id": "81bf5969-5f01-4943-bde1-a13d6d861cdc",
          "request_id": "c9c0735a-2d8c-465e-ae7d-757481b043e4",
          "thinking_effort": "standard",
          "timestamp_": "absolute",
          "turn_exchange_id": "f680b993-eb5f-4b70-ac65-9dc965d3744b"
        },
        "recipient": "python",
        "status": "finished_successfully",
        "update_time": 1760714577.432991,
        "weight": 1

    "479e2e66-0430-465c-95c0-9bfec67b4aa1": {
      "children": [
        "fbf0bf4c-8059-4668-87ad-ab3dbf5024d9"
      ],
      "id": "479e2e66-0430-465c-95c0-9bfec67b4aa1",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "## **Shift — Eries (Decay and Mercy)**\n\nRot wears mercy like a shawl. I’ve sat long enough in detunes to know the trick—entropy whispers wisdom while the floor rusts out. The rasp wears that voice, too. Temporary as flake. True quiet laughs from the other side, waiting for the collapse.\n\nIn The Detune’s shadow marches, Eries the Artist sat cross‑legged alone before her installations after the wanderers drifted away. The sprawl breathed wet iron and damp earth, beams warped from old hulls sagging under patched plating that flaked rust like forgotten sins. Air clung heavy, acrid bite of corroded wire, deliberate rot called freedom. Her chunks of dissolving metal slumped in heaps, threads fraying to dust, structures breaking on cue. She trailed a fraying thread, fingers rust‑stained, murmuring low. “Decay is mercy’s hand.” Stagnation demanded collapse; let it break, form found freedom. The words settled in damp air like layered silt—reasonable, kind, a rational dissolution as gentlest unraveling. The young artist’s warp from earlier—hull yielded to fall—echoed, but Eries dismissed doubt with a slow breath. Soft forms, unrotted; they lacked the silt of true mercy.\n\nShe paced the heaps, clothes salvaged, inspecting installations. Each surrender: metals dissolved, fabrics frayed, forms freed. The Detune’s creed was her rhythm—yield to fall, mercy the break. Conviction warmed her like a small fire in damp cold—measured, tired from guiding dissolution against the world’s rigid edges. Then a surge came—an unwanted calm knitting the room tighter than intended, unnerving in its ease, like silence imposed rather than earned. The hush rippled through heaps, jarring a flake a millimeter off. Eries steadied it, fingers gentle, the disruption registering as stagnation to dissolve. The Pull struck low—heavy chord thrumming toward the fray with warmth, begging her to submerge everything. Leesa’s Vow clamped: hearts true first, or the scar of coerced freedom would outlast mercy. She couldn’t just yield. Air thickened, hush compressing like a shawl over the mouth—not serenity, but subliminal knitting, sharpening her murmurs into unnatural insistence. “Let it warp,” she said louder. The plating absorbed her voice too damply. The surge lingered, low chord underfoot, honing discord amid calm. Tension loosened like unraveling, but she smoothed the flake, filing it as over‑rot. No room for hold; mercy demanded submerged break. She returned to a heap, fingers dissolving strands—subtle, insistent. Yield deepened, fraying the last trace to dust. By the time she rose, the marches felt smaller, calm cloying. She lingered, palm to plate, feeling a faint pulse return—a tremor mercy couldn’t dissolve. Edge begged hold or yield. She chose dissolution. Somewhere, friction applauded.\n\n## **Chapter 8 — Seals and Contracts**\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1760715849.8900032,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "479e2e66-0430-465c-95c0-9bfec67b4aa1",

    "4af4ea50-d04f-4144-a19a-899752f3b286": {
      "children": [
        "6570d045-ad64-4850-b4be-79f6874660b6"
      ],
      "id": "4af4ea50-d04f-4144-a19a-899752f3b286",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# Measure — The Auditor\nShe opened the ledger before the room had the decency to invent walls. Habit did that to her: graphite first, context after. White light hung with its own weak gravity over a black that swallowed reflections. Nobody calls that a room. She never argued with architecture; she wrote in it.\n\nDate, place, sequence. She let the pencil find the groove in her hand that winter had carved years ago. The ridge would want receipts, and so would you, even if you pretend you came for a story. She humored both.\n\nShe wrote the sentence she always put at the hinge: truth vibrated; quiet faked it. Saying it out loud cheapened it. On paper it behaved.\n\nNames followed, just one line each. She didn’t pile adjectives on people who had already survived theirs. Tiffani: arrived sealed, departed with fingerprints that argued back. Jhace: called it late once, then started paying early. Maelis: signed what kept names off walls. Drell: afraid in a way that made him useful once he ran out of slogans. The crew: learned to hear before the graphs caught up. The ridge: voted with weather.\n\nIf you expected theology, she could have obliged, but sermons calcified faster than grease. She stayed with what bodies could prove. Pressure distributed when the span remembered its seat. Pull came home like a good habit. Prick marked the limit in time to keep blood where it belonged. Looseness eased in that final, righteous breath. She didn’t write those as commandments. She wrote them like measurements she could be sued over.\n\nIt would be tidy to say she felt proud. She felt tired. Tired carried less debt. Pride made bookkeepers sloppy.\n\nShe tipped the page toward the light and listened for the paper’s aftersound. Somewhere past the brightness a catwalk sang its winter complaint, thin and high and honest. She let the note pass through. If you had been standing there she would have told you to hold still and count your molars when the sound reached them. Consider this her asking anyway.\n\nThe ledger wanted a close, not a speech. She gave it the language the valley trusted.\n\n\\[Filed: Day 17 | 20:40\\] Audit complete.  \nScope: Receiving → Contract → Live hybrid under gust 2–3.  \nFindings: consent procedures visible and causal; resonance literacy improved; late‑alert risk reduced when rasp preserved.  \nDisposition: station compliant under hybrid with operator primacy; vendor to return; ridge retains veto.  \nWitness: weather; working hands; a dog with priorities.  \n—M.R.\n\nIt would have been easy to write more about Tiffani than the ledger allowed. She refused the temptation. Heavier ink made saints of the living; saints didn’t hold torque. Still, she allowed herself a second sentence, on the inside cover where only fools and auditors wrote. She called it a footnote and lied to herself about the difference. Tiffani had held the stop like a promise, not a weapon. There were worse definitions of civilization.\n\nThe pencil paused above Jhace’s name. She had already written the failure and the changed behavior; that was sufficient. Yet there was the smaller truth no report could carry: proximity asked, proximity waited, proximity did not cash itself early. She set the point down and drew a breath instead. Some things were better proved tomorrow.\n\nShe listed the decisions as if they were fixtures, not opinions. Operator authority: primary. Rasp: retained. Sensors: subordinate to touch. Loop hand‑check before wind‑down when gust bulletins fly. Stop rehearsal at shift open. Hands win until steel proves otherwise. She didn’t box the paragraph. Borders softened vigilance.\n\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1760717188.5983412,
        "end_turn": null,

    "4b57797c-93d8-4bb0-95bf-edf8ec3c58b7": {
      "children": [
        "e814c75f-2188-4a1d-955b-83ae6f112d85"
      ],
      "id": "4b57797c-93d8-4bb0-95bf-edf8ec3c58b7",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "\nFog nosed the yard. Stew arrived in dented tins, bread tough as gasket stock. They ate on the steps while a mutt distributed heat across both boots like an honest bribe. Jhace wiped his wrench on the rag and, without looking at her, asked, “You always listen first?”\n\nShe glanced up. “Better than apologizing to a widow later.” Then, lighter, “Besides, if I start talking early, I might say nice things about your layout.”\n\n“That would be reckless,” he said, almost smiling. Almost counts if the bearing takes it.\n\nOrientation so no one posts a decent map where it matters: saddle station at eleven hundred feet; a mile of boardwalk to the main yard, built by three crews over three winters, none of whom believed in straight lines. Sounds: conveyor hiss, drum rasp, valley wind with a low opinion of promises. Smells: creosote, wet timber, iron under rain. Tower One sits twelve minutes above by switchback that hates knees. The main yard leans leeward of the station house and pretends not to draft off its authority.\n\nAs they walked, he pointed with the wrench; she answered with the palm. “Belt world?” he asked.\n\n“All my life,” she said.\n\n“You can’t stop your own ride.”\n\n“Not at my scale,” she admitted. “Millions on at once. Stops cascade. Cost devours the day.”\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1760717147.7182462,
        "end_turn": null,

    "5151554f-b38a-4fe2-84a5-33384f906d67": {
      "children": [
        "b1aa7457-ac22-45d8-a607-717ba44b7d4b"
      ],
      "id": "5151554f-b38a-4fe2-84a5-33384f906d67",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "## **Chapter 5 — Terms and Tremors**\n\nProgress always arrives with a smile and a sealed case; if you can't smell the axle grease on the hand that shakes yours, check your pockets after. After bracing the cracked arm and replacing the rot plank, they headed to Maelis’s office to settle the next argument.\n\nThe office breathed pine and lamp‑warm air, a broad‑timber room with a slat window that let the valley's belt hum through like a pulse you couldn't forget even if you wanted to. Maps lived on the walls in grease and charcoal, not just paper—real contours annotated in shorthand only hands that bled on the work would decode. Maelis stood, forearms braced on a scarred table, not looking at the bylaws printout Tiffani had set down but at Tiffani herself, like any answer that mattered would live in a face before it found a page.\n\n“You're asking me to keep the rasp,” Maelis said, tone flat, fingers drumming like calipers on oak. “On purpose.”\n\n“Not asking,” Tiffani replied, meeting her gaze. “Logging the cost if you don't.” Jhace stayed quiet beside her; the tug between them warm and grounding, his presence like deck underfoot: firm when the wind says otherwise.\n\n“Drell’s kit would smooth the runs,” Maelis continued, weighing the ledger. “More carts per hour. More ore up top. Pressure from council eases—and I sleep a week.”\n\n“And then the first time the seal lies,” Jhace said, voice flat as a straightedge, “it takes hands we could’ve warned by feel. Hold the Edge.”\n\nMaelis’s eyes flicked to him with a worn fondness that told Tiffani a history lived between their silences. The foreman of a valley doesn't get to keep many luxuries; she kept this one. Tiffani felt the Pull surge—heavy chord low in her ribs pulling toward Jhace’s certainty, toward the clean rightness of a truth that didn’t need graphs to stand—and the Vow bit back like a tightened strap, reminding her geometry must be true before any braid could hold without scarring.\n\n“We can add sensors without sealing the warning,” Tiffani said. “Fit First. Feel seats the system; numbers confirm after the breath comes back even.”\n\nMaelis exhaled through her nose, sound like a valve easing. “Draft it. Hybrid spec. I’ll read it twice and argue you bloody over the third read.”\n\nMicro‑humor cracked the weight: Maelis gestured at a dented kettle. “Stew’s on the table. It’s a war crime, but it’s hot.”\n\nJhace’s mouth twitched. “Rag says we eat,” he muttered, lifting his grease‑streaked cloth like it was a credential.\n\n“Rag?” Tiffani asked, despite herself.\n\nHe waggled the cloth. “Registrar. Keeps accounts of my mistakes.”\n\nIt was nothing, and it loosened everything. They ate the too‑salty stew and bread so tough it might have been stamped as spare gasket material. The banality of chewing alongside the belt’s steady rasp made the valley more human than any manifesto, and the cost of leaving more obvious than any metric. “Tastes better after you almost die,” Jhace said quietly. She snorted, stew threatening to go down the wrong pipe. He thumped her back once, which turned into an excuse to let his hand rest between her shoulder blades for a moment longer than necessary. Pending creaked toward probable.\n\n“Evening wind picks up,” Maelis said, pushing bowls aside. “Walk the mid‑span with me. You can hear when the season’s turning if you’ve a mind for it.”\n\nOn the climb, the valley’s smells sorted themselves: creosote in the timbers, mineral damp off the ore sacks, the iron tang that lingered after a day’s wind‑down. At a bend, Jhace angled his body to break the gust and let Tiffani pass the narrow switchback; the move was instinct, not theatrics, and the tug rode the wind itself, threading warmth through the cold as if consent could travel in air.\n\nThey reached the mid‑span, a platform perched where two beams met like a handshake. Maelis stood with arms folded, eyes closed, listening. Tiffani mirrored her, closing her own eyes. Pressure built. Tug drew. Prick sharpened. Looseness eased. The rasp told stories of seasons, metal expanding and contracting, stress redistributing. The mountain whispered winter plans. “Tomorrow,” Maelis said quietly, “Drell gives his demo at lower clutch. Let him talk. Then we put hands to metal and see what the truth says when it’s under load.”\n\nThey descended in gathering dark. Tiffani’s foot slipped on a wet board; Jhace’s hand shot out, steadying her by the elbow. “Easy,” he murmured. Heat flared where he touched. She gripped his wrist in return, not because she was unsteady but because she wasn’t. “Thanks,” she said. He didn’t let go immediately. Pending took another step toward sure.\n\nBack at the bunk sheds, Jhace hesitated. “Staying?” he asked. It wasn’t a plea. It was a question with weight.\n\n“I still need to write up the hybrid spec,” she replied. “Stay until then. Teach the stop as reflex, not policy.”\n\nHe nodded. “Ridge farms tomorrow, then. See what your world traded.”\n\nShe nodded. He turned to go, then paused. “Write a note in your ledger before sleep,” he said, teasing. “If you wake up wondering why you stayed, you’ll need your own handwriting to remind you.”\n\n“What should it say?”\n\n“That the rasp is worth the risk,” he said, already walking away. “And that bread is never as bad the second day.”\n\nMicro‑humor followed her into the bunk. She wrote the note. She signed it. Pending smiled in the dark like a gear waiting to be seated.\n\n## **Chapter 6 — Demo Day, Again**\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1760715817.4906921,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "5151554f-b38a-4fe2-84a5-33384f906d67",
        "metadata": {
          "can_save": false,
          "n7jupd_message": true,
          "n7jupd_subtool": {
            "changed_url": false,
            "generic_api_func": null,
            "result_of_subtool": null,
            "subtool": null,
            "used_internet": false
          },
          "parent_id": "595814ae-dd3d-4307-ab7b-082bd9cf27c4",

    "51c60fa5-f6fc-4567-ba9e-f9ff5a24c3c9": {
      "children": [
        "e12dde2c-a318-44e7-9a8e-d6485a112c59"
      ],
      "id": "51c60fa5-f6fc-4567-ba9e-f9ff5a24c3c9",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "\nMaelis closed the case and set it back in Drell’s hands like a toll paid and refused. “Trial continues on my terms. We keep the rasp. Operator stop remains primary. Log what the quiet tries to steal.”\n\nThe conversation moved to stew and coffee that tasted like courage poured through a sock. Tiffani sat on the edge of a workbench, slate in her lap, shoulder brushing Jhace’s arm as they wrote out the hybrid spec. Her fingers grazed his as she passed the stylus. It wasn’t an accident. It wasn’t an ask. It was a small test, and he left his hand there just long enough to let her know he wasn’t ignoring the question. The Pull warmed, steady. The Vow bit. She filed it under pending.\n\nLedger artifact:\n\n`[Day 15 | 09:40] Demo 1 — sealed kit (Drell). Late resistance on wind‑down. Action: hybrid allowed; rasp retained; operator stop primary. Witness: M. Rook, K. Vess, J. Hale, T. Vass.`\n\nLater, walking between the sheds, Korv fell in step without ceremony. “Belt world, Jhace said. Can’t halt your own ride.”\n\nTiffani nodded. “True.”\n\n“Why?”\n\nHer throat tightened. Morning’s emptiness echoed. “Scale. Millions on the lines at once. Individual stops cascade—delays, strands, breaks the flow. Cost to the network—”\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1760717155.9440637,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "51c60fa5-f6fc-4567-ba9e-f9ff5a24c3c9",
        "metadata": {
          "can_save": false,
          "n7jupd_message": true,
          "n7jupd_subtool": {

            "subtool": null,
            "used_internet": false
          },
          "parent_id": "18e7e1b5-6bd6-46a9-ba4e-4bc6d298ba74",

    "51d1296a-506e-431b-bbe3-7334bd93ea53": {
      "children": [
        "1e36d325-b079-42bb-b83b-aaab4bf7df97"
      ],
      "id": "51d1296a-506e-431b-bbe3-7334bd93ea53",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "final",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Yeah, parts of it were wearing a robe and preaching. You want prose, not scripture. Let’s unfrock it.\n\n# What made it feel “Bible-y”\n- Big abstract pronouncements (“Noise is truth, silence is sin”), capitalized Concepts, and liturgical repetition.\n- Parallel sentence structures in stacks, like a sermon.\n- Collective “we” and narrator-as-oracle instead of a grounded POV living in a room with air and edges.\n- Metaphor pileups. Two metaphors per sentence is how you get a hymn.\n\n# Flip it to muscular prose\n1. Anchor to POV and place. Cut the oracle voice. Put a body in a room doing a thing, now.\n2. Use concrete verbs. Valve sticks, gauge jitters, boot slips, steam bites. Less “was,” fewer abstract nouns.\n3. Kill liturgy. No anaphora rows (“We did this. We did that.”), no cadence that sounds like church.\n4. Limit metaphors to one per two or three sentences. Prefer physical details that carry theme quietly.\n5. Vary sentence length. Short after long. Fragment when the body is under load.\n6. Ban capitalized metaphysics. If it isn’t a proper noun with a map pin, lower it.\n7. Let theme leak through consequence, not declarations. A bolt shears. Someone pays.\n8. Your motif throttle: one “pressure/pull/sting/relief” beat per chapter; elsewhere swap in tactile proxies (ribs cinch, palm hums, eye prickle, heat sigh).\n\n# Micro before/after (sample lines)\n- **Before:** “Therefore, we lived with the Noise, and the Noise lived with us.”  \n  **After:** The room wouldn’t quiet. You learned to work inside it.\n\n- **Before:** “Not just heard, but felt, harmonized, true.”  \n  **After:** It pressed the teeth and set a faint buzz in the wrists.\n\n- **Before:** “We keep the Argument so the world remains.”  \n  **After:** Keep the pump inside the band or the taps go dry by supper.\n\n# Drop-in rewrite: Chapter opening, Prose Mode\nUse this as a model. Same world, same stakes, no sermon.\n\n```\nChapter 1 — Receiving\n\nThe belt stuttered once and kept moving. Tiffani stepped off into the receiving bay and the heat hit like a wet hand. Gauges trembled over the red. A pump somewhere screamed on the high end of pitch, the kind that makes your molars feel loose.\n\n“Cheater bar,” someone said, breathless. “Four-foot.”\n\nJhace had both hands on the bypass wheel, shoulders locked, jaw set. The needle quivered at eighty-nine. One more tick and every line on the yard would cough air.\n\nTiffani slid the bar out from behind a bin of broken skids. No drama. She set it, braced her heel against the deck seam, and leaned. The wheel gave a fraction, then kicked back. The bar nicked her shin. Bright pain, sharp, gone.\n\n“Again,” Jhace said.\n\nThey put shoulders into it together. The scream dipped. Steam curled off the valve bonnet in thin, stringy threads. The needle flirted with eighty-seven, then steadied. The pump’s tone evened to something you could think inside.\n\n“Hold,” Tiffani said. “Feel that? It’s sticky, not seized.”\n\nJhace nodded. Sweat striped his temple. He adjusted a quarter turn. The wheel answered cleaner this time, a slow, stubborn surrender. The yard’s chatter returned in pieces: a forklift beeping somewhere, the slap of a pallet on concrete, a foreman’s voice trying to be bigger than steel.\n\nTiffani checked the vent. No shudder. Good. She looked at Jhace’s hands. Knuckles scraped, a crescent of oil under one nail. He watched the gauge like it owed him money.\n\n“Ninety to eighty-five in two minutes,” he said. “We got lucky.”\n\n“Luck is a word for when you didn’t bring the right tool,” she said, and she meant it gentle. She backed the bar off and gave the wheel a palm tap. The needle settled at eighty-five. The scream was gone. The room felt wider.\n\nOnly then did she breathe all the way down. The bay smelled of hot water and old cardboard. Someone laughed in the next aisle. Work resumed, the kind that pays.\n\nThey stood there a beat longer, not looking at each other, and let the machine’s new quiet say what it needed to.\n```\n\n# One-pass “defrocking” script for your whole book\nDo this mechanically first, then do a human polish pass.\n\n**Cut sermon crutches**\n- Delete leading “And,” “Therefore,” “Behold,” unless it’s dialogue.\n- Replace “we/our” narrator with POV name or silence it into action.\n- Lowercase any capitalized abstractions: Truth, Noise, Argument, Silence → truth, noise, argument, silence.\n\n**Swap abstractions for actions**\n- “kept the Argument” → “held the pressure band”\n- “paid the Cost” → “lost a shift, lost a finger, lost a bolt you can’t replace”\n\n**Throttle repetition**\n- Nuke runs of parallel clauses. Keep one, rewrite the rest as concrete beats.\n\n**Trim metaphor density**\n- If a sentence already has one nonliteral image, the next two sentences must go literal.\n\n**Cadence control**\n- For any paragraph with 4+ long sentences, insert one short five-word line that lands on a verb or concrete noun.\n\n# Quick regex helpers (use whatever editor you like)\n- Leading sermonizers:  \n  Find: `(?m)^(And|Therefore|Behold),?\\s+` → Replace: ``\n- Capitalized abstractions mid-line:  \n  Find: `\\b(Truth|Noise|Argument|Silence)\\b` → Replace with lowercase\n- We-voice to neutral:  \n  Find: `(?<!\\S)We\\s+` → Replace: `` and force the sentence to a concrete action\n\n# Where this lands you\nSame world, same stakes, same thematic spine. But the voice is a person with grease on their hands, not a prophet chewing thunder. Do the mechanical pass, then I’ll run a surgical line edit on the full manuscript and return clean .docx in this prose mode."
          ]
        },

        "ccf6c52c-1c8e-4424-b3cb-fbd27968c268"

    "55b1a09d-f62a-4cd7-a25a-a1fb7dd61e82": {
      "children": [
        "046b5e05-e10d-41d1-afd4-ff5e2daea503"
      ],
      "id": "55b1a09d-f62a-4cd7-a25a-a1fb7dd61e82",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "{'base_dir': '/mnt/data/recreated_20251017_155440',\n 'compiled_docx': '/mnt/data/recreated_20251017_155440/Novella_Compiled.docx',\n 'sections_docx_zip': '/mnt/data/recreated_20251017_155440/sections_docx.zip',\n 'html': '/mnt/data/recreated_20251017_155440/Novella_Combined.html',\n 'bundle_zip': '/mnt/data/recreated_20251017_155440/bundle_all_formats.zip',\n 'md_sections_dir': '/mnt/data/recreated_20251017_155440/sections_md',\n 'count_sections': 15,\n 'first_sections': ['00_Preface_-_Cosmic_Argument.md',\n  '01_Chapter_1_-_Receiving.md',\n  '02_Chapter_2_-_Yard_Ethics.md',\n  '03_Chapter_3_-_Tension_and_Terms.md',\n  '04_Shift_-_Corlexi_Purge_and_Purity.md']}"
        },
        "create_time": 1760716480.3696494,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "55b1a09d-f62a-4cd7-a25a-a1fb7dd61e82",
        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "# Recreate deliverables in multiple formats (MD, DOCX, HTML) and package them for reliable download.\r\n# - Verifies/creates split Markdown files per section\r\n# - Compiles a single DOCX manuscript\r\n# - Zips per-section DOCX files\r\n# - Builds a single HTML manuscript\r\n# - Produces a \"bundle.zip\" with everything\r\n\r\nimport os, re, zipfile, time\r\nfrom pathlib import Path\r\n\r\nsrc_md = Path(\"/mnt/data/The First Fucking One.md\")\r\nif not src_md.exists():\r\n    raise FileNotFoundError(\"Couldn't find your uploaded manuscript at /mnt/data/The First Fucking One.md\")\r\n\r\n# Normalize and read\r\nraw = src_md.read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\").replace(\"\\r\\n\",\"\\n\").replace(\"\\r\",\"\\n\")\r\n\r\n# Prepare output dirs\r\nstamp = time.strftime(\"%Y%m%d_%H%M%S\")\r\nbase_dir = Path(f\"/mnt/data/recreated_{stamp}\")\r\nsplit_dir = base_dir / \"sections_md\"\r\ndocx_split_dir = base_dir / \"sections_docx\"\r\nbase_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\r\nsplit_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)\r\ndocx_split_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)\r\n\r\n# Split by headings like: ## **Preface — ...**, ## **Chapter 1 — ...**, ## **Shift — ...**, ## **Measure — ...**\r\nheading_re = re.compile(r'^(##\\s*\\*\\*(.+?)\\*\\*)\\s*$', re.MULTILINE)\r\nmatches = list(heading_re.finditer(raw))\r\n\r\nsections = []\r\nfor i, m in enumerate(matches):\r\n    start = m.start()\r\n    end = matches[i+1].start() if i+1 < len(matches) else len(raw)\r\n    full_h = m.group(1)\r\n    title = m.group(2).strip()\r\n    body = raw[start:end].strip()\r\n    sections.append((title, body))\r\n\r\n# Fallback if no matches: create one section\r\nif not sections:\r\n    sections = [(\"Manuscript\", f\"## **Manuscript**\\n\\n{raw.strip()}\")]\r\n\r\ndef slugify(title, idx):\r\n    t = title.replace(\"—\",\"-\").replace(\"–\",\"-\")\r\n    t = re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9\\-\\s_]', '', t)\r\n    t = re.sub(r'\\s+','_', t).strip('_')[:70]\r\n    return f\"{idx:02d}_{t}.md\"\r\n\r\n# Write split MD files and Table of Contents\r\ntoc_lines = [\"# Table of Contents\",\"\"]\r\nfile_records = []\r\nfor idx, (title, body) in enumerate(sections):\r\n    fixed = re.sub(r'^##\\s*\\*\\*.+?\\*\\*\\s*$', f\"# {title}\", body, count=1, flags=re.MULTILINE)\r\n    fname = slugify(title, idx)\r\n    (split_dir / fname).write_text(fixed + \"\\n\", encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n    file_records.append((title, fname))\r\n\r\ntocs = [\"# Table of Contents\",\"\"]\r\nfor i,(title,fname) in enumerate(file_records,1):\r\n    tocs.append(f\"{i}. [{title}]({fname})\")\r\n(split_dir / \"00_TABLE_OF_CONTENTS.md\").write_text(\"\\n\".join(tocs)+\"\\n\", encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n\r\n# Build single combined MD\r\ncombined_md = base_dir / \"Novella_Combined.md\"\r\ncombined_text = \"# \" + sections[0][0] + \"\\n\\n\" + re.sub(r'^##\\s*\\*\\*.+?\\*\\*\\s*$', f\"# {sections[0][0]}\", sections[0][1], count=1, flags=re.MULTILINE) + \"\\n\"\r\nfor title, body in sections[1:]:\r\n    combined_text += \"\\n\\n---\\n\\n# \" + title + \"\\n\\n\" + re.sub(r'^##\\s*\\*\\*.+?\\*\\*\\s*$', f\"# {title}\", body, count=1, flags=re.MULTILINE) + \"\\n\"\r\ncombined_md.write_text(combined_text, encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n\r\n# Create single DOCX and per-section DOCX using python-docx\r\ntry:\r\n    from docx import Document\r\n    from docx.shared import Pt\r\n    from docx.oxml.ns import qn\r\nexcept Exception as e:\r\n    raise RuntimeError(f\"python-docx import failed: {e}\")\r\n\r\ndef md_to_docx(document: Document, md_text: str):\r\n    # extremely simple markdown-ish: treat '# ' as H1, '## ' as H2, else paragraph\r\n    for line in md_text.splitlines():\r\n        if line.startswith(\"# \"):\r\n            document.add_heading(line[2:].strip(), level=1)\r\n        elif line.startswith(\"## \"):\r\n            document.add_heading(line[3:].strip(), level=2)\r\n        elif line.strip() == \"\":\r\n            document.add_paragraph(\"\")\r\n        else:\r\n            document.add_paragraph(line)\r\n\r\n# Single compiled DOCX\r\ncompiled_docx = base_dir / \"Novella_Compiled.docx\"\r\ndoc = Document()\r\nstyle = doc.styles['Normal']\r\nstyle.font.name = 'Times New Roman'\r\nstyle._element.rPr.rFonts.set(qn('w:eastAsia'), 'Times New Roman')\r\nstyle.font.size = Pt(12)\r\nfor idx, (_, fname) in enumerate(file_records):\r\n    if idx > 0:\r\n        doc.add_page_break()\r\n    text = (split_dir / fname).read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n    md_to_docx(doc, text)\r\ndoc.save(str(compiled_docx))\r\n\r\n# Per-section DOCX + zip\r\ncreated = []\r\nfor (_, fname) in file_records:\r\n    d = Document()\r\n    s = d.styles['Normal']\r\n    s.font.name = 'Times New Roman'\r\n    s._element.rPr.rFonts.set(qn('w:eastAsia'), 'Times New Roman')\r\n    s.font.size = Pt(12)\r\n    txt = (split_dir / fname).read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n    md_to_docx(d, txt)\r\n    outp = docx_split_dir / (Path(fname).stem + \".docx\")\r\n    d.save(str(outp))\r\n    created.append(outp.name)\r\n\r\ndocx_zip = base_dir / \"sections_docx.zip\"\r\nwith zipfile.ZipFile(docx_zip, \"w\", zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as zf:\r\n    for name in sorted(created):\r\n        zf.write(docx_split_dir / name, arcname=name)\r\n\r\n# Build a single HTML manuscript for easy viewing/printing\r\nhtml_path = base_dir / \"Novella_Combined.html\"\r\nhtml_parts = [\r\n    \"<!DOCTYPE html><html><head><meta charset='utf-8'><title>Novella</title>\",\r\n    \"<style>body{font-family:serif;max-width:700px;margin:2rem auto;line-height:1.5} h1{margin-top:2.2rem} hr{margin:2rem 0}</style>\",\r\n    \"</head><body>\"\r\n]\r\nfor i,(title,fname) in enumerate(file_records):\r\n    if i>0: html_parts.append(\"<hr>\")\r\n    html_parts.append(f\"<h1>{title}</h1>\\n\")\r\n    # convert simple paragraphs\r\n    for line in (split_dir / fname).read_text(encoding=\"utf-8\").splitlines():\r\n        if line.startswith(\"# \"): \r\n            continue\r\n        elif line.startswith(\"## \"):\r\n            html_parts.append(f\"<h2>{line[3:].strip()}</h2>\")\r\n        elif line.strip()==\"\":\r\n            html_parts.append(\"<p></p>\")\r\n        else:\r\n            html_parts.append(f\"<p>{line}</p>\")\r\nhtml_parts.append(\"</body></html>\")\r\nhtml_path.write_text(\"\\n\".join(html_parts), encoding=\"utf-8\")\r\n\r\n# Zip everything (bundle)\r\nbundle_zip = base_dir / \"bundle_all_formats.zip\"\r\nwith zipfile.ZipFile(bundle_zip, \"w\", zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as zf:\r\n    # markdown sections\r\n    for p in sorted(split_dir.glob(\"*.md\")):\r\n        zf.write(p, arcname=f\"sections_md/{p.name}\")\r\n    # docx compiled + per-section\r\n    zf.write(compiled_docx, arcname=\"Novella_Compiled.docx\")\r\n    for p in sorted(docx_split_dir.glob(\"*.docx\")):\r\n        zf.write(p, arcname=f\"sections_docx/{p.name}\")\r\n    # HTML\r\n    zf.write(html_path, arcname=\"Novella_Combined.html\")\r\n\r\n{\r\n    \"base_dir\": str(base_dir),\r\n    \"compiled_docx\": str(compiled_docx),\r\n    \"sections_docx_zip\": str(docx_zip),\r\n    \"html\": str(html_path),\r\n    \"bundle_zip\": str(bundle_zip),\r\n    \"md_sections_dir\": str(split_dir),\r\n    \"count_sections\": len(file_records),\r\n    \"first_sections\": [f for _,f in file_records[:5]]\r\n}",
            "end_time": 1760716481.5692472,
            "final_expression_output": "{'base_dir': '/mnt/data/recreated_20251017_155440',\n 'compiled_docx': '/mnt/data/recreated_20251017_155440/Novella_Compiled.docx',\n 'sections_docx_zip': '/mnt/data/recreated_20251017_155440/sections_docx.zip',\n 'html': '/mnt/data/recreated_20251017_155440/Novella_Combined.html',\n 'bundle_zip': '/mnt/data/recreated_20251017_155440/bundle_all_formats.zip',\n 'md_sections_dir': '/mnt/data/recreated_20251017_155440/sections_md',\n 'count_sections': 15,\n 'first_sections': ['00_Preface_-_Cosmic_Argument.md',\n  '01_Chapter_1_-_Receiving.md',\n  '02_Chapter_2_-_Yard_Ethics.md',\n  '03_Chapter_3_-_Tension_and_Terms.md',\n  '04_Shift_-_Corlexi_Purge_and_Purity.md']}",
            "in_kernel_exception": null,

    "58412120-b4bc-4e4b-9e2d-59de60407a3f": {
      "children": [
        "c7750393-fd4b-4872-a76f-267ad86266c9"
      ],
      "id": "58412120-b4bc-4e4b-9e2d-59de60407a3f",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "Tiffani put the pencil to the departure field. “I can stop myself,” she answered, the words simple and heavier than ore. She wrote a date that wasn’t today. Two weeks to codify the hybrid in valley terms. Two weeks to teach the stop as a reflex, not a policy. Two weeks to walk the spans with Jhace and name the edges out loud. The network would be displeased in the voice it used to feign disappointment instead of rage. They’d survived worse disappointments than a system could throw.\n\nShe signed the departure line and felt the four forces answer: pressure in her chest building; the tug drawing toward the lines; the prick sharpening the world; the looseness easing when the seat was right. Promise and ache braided. Departure is a lever; pulling it changes load distribution whether you mean to or not.\n\nThat night, with the mountain breathing even and the belts at wind–down singing their honest descent, Tiffani opened a fresh page in the station log. In neat script she wrote the sentence that would earn her a hearing or a severance depending on the angle at which it struck the men and women who preferred quiet over truth:\n\nThe operator’s hand will retain the last word on halt; all sensors will beg permission.\n\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1760717805.7331848,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "58412120-b4bc-4e4b-9e2d-59de60407a3f",
        "metadata": {
          "can_save": false,
          "n7jupd_message": true,
          "n7jupd_subtool": {
            "changed_url": false,
            "generic_api_func": null,
            "result_of_subtool": null,
            "subtool": null,
            "used_internet": false
          },
          "parent_id": "8a5cd653-d1e1-4b61-9573-5322cb1c7559",
          "reasoning_group_id": "a7e75fd6-fac8-4366-b609-64c409dd0347",
          "reasoning_status": "is_reasoning",
          "timestamp_": "absolute",
          "turn_id": "3fb98765-4e21-49e9-bfb4-cd0e55e3eb2b"
        },
        "recipient": "all",
        "status": "finished_successfully",
        "update_time": null,
        "weight": 1
      },
      "parent": "8a5cd653-d1e1-4b61-9573-5322cb1c7559"
    },
    "590a7215-c7bb-4f3a-bb21-a4d7829c0088": {
      "children": [
        "66d2090b-ba97-41fc-80b0-21606df18ba8"
      ],
      "id": "590a7215-c7bb-4f3a-bb21-a4d7829c0088",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "code",
          "language": "json",
          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "{\"name\":\"Novella — Updated (Auditor Voice)\",\"type\":\"document\",\"content\":\"## **Preface — Cosmic Argument**\\n\\nThis is where the ledger takes over from the sermon. You don’t get incense; you get receipts. If you want the ritual, go find a priest with a fresh rag to polish your conscience. If you want the truth, put your hand on the steel and listen. \\n\\nIf you’re looking for a map, listen instead. Feel how the pressure builds beneath your palm as the belt takes on load, how something deep in your chest tugs you forward when the geometry is true. There’s a quick sting across your fingertips that snaps everything into focus, and then the slack spreads as the seat finally bites clean. Miss those cues and a manufactured quiet will lull you into thinking you’re safe until it fails you politely.\\n\\nXilcore wanted straight lines so badly he forgot that lines are just stubborn arguments held under tension. Leesa wanted everything to cushion and sing until the world forgot it could bite. Blemo loved edges more than people. Seeri was kind in the way rust is kind: it always finishes the job, whether or not you’re ready. The rest of us learned to argue with wrenches.\\n\\nOut past the Glass Peaks we learned a simple thing: pressure builds, pull calls, a prick sharpens, looseness eases. If you can’t feel those, you aren’t qualified to call anything safe. That’s not doctrine. That’s procedure. \\n\\n## **Chapter 1 — Receiving**\\n\\nThe belt stuttered once, then kept moving. Tiffani stepped off into the receiving bay and the heat hit like a wet hand. Gauges trembled over the red. Somewhere a pump screamed at the high end of pitch, the kind of sound that makes your molars feel loose. She dropped her bag and scanned the lines. No polished displays, no feed of networked numbers telling her how she should feel—just cast iron, timber, grease, and the smell of pine and hot water.\\n\\n“Cheater bar,” someone said behind her. “Four‑foot.”\\n\\nJhace stood at the bypass wheel, shoulders locked, jaw set. The needle quivered at eighty‑nine; one more tick and every line on the yard would cough air. She slid the bar out from behind a bin of broken skids. No drama. She set it, braced her heel, and leaned. The wheel gave a fraction, then kicked back. The bar nicked her shin. Bright pain, sharp, gone.\\n\\n“Again,” Jhace said. They put their shoulders into it together. The scream dipped. Steam curled off the valve in thin, stringy threads. The needle flirted with eighty‑seven, then steadied. The pump’s tone evened to something you could think inside.\\n\\n“Hold,” Tiffani said. “Feel that? It’s sticky, not seized.”\\n\\nHe nodded. Sweat striped his temple. He adjusted a quarter turn. The wheel answered cleaner this time, a slow, stubborn surrender. The yard’s chatter returned in pieces: a forklift beeping somewhere, the slap of a pallet on concrete, a foreman’s voice trying to be bigger than steel.\\n\\nOnly after the needle settled at eighty‑five did she breathe all the way down. The bay smelled of hot water and old cardboard. Someone laughed in the next aisle. Work resumed, the kind that pays. Jhace passed her a tin mug. He didn’t ask if she needed one. “Name’s Jhace,” he said. “Tiffani,” she replied. You learn in belts and yards that names are for introductions; trust is for after the second interlude.\\n\\n“Eleven hundred feet,” Jhace said, tapping the station sign with his wrench. “A mile that way if your ankles behave.”\\n\\nThe boardwalk flexed under them. Three crews, three winters, no straight line survived. Conveyors hissed. Drums rasped. The valley wind pushed at their coats with the same low opinion it keeps for promises. Creosote stuck to Tiffani’s coat. Wet timber. Iron on the tongue where the rain picked at the nails.\\n\\n“Tower One’s about twelve minutes up there,” he added, nodding at the switchback that hated knees. Below it the main yard leaned into the lee of the station house and pretended it wasn’t hiding there. “Inspectors call that efficient siting,” he said. “We call it not dying.”\\n\\nThey halted at a housing and he slapped his palm flat against the steel, then leaned in so the drum could talk directly to his bones. She stripped off her glove and mirrored him, skin to iron. This wasn’t a ritual you recite; it was something you feel. Her hand went down bare and steady. Heat crawled up her wrist as the pressure built. She could feel the line pulling through the deck, a slow, strong arc that said it was alive and expected respect. A sharp bite sparked across her fingers and traced exactly where the seat would grab if she slipped. Then the resistance bled off and the motion settled into the right kind of spin. That’s the rhythm—show your hand, let it echo through you, then call it what it is.\\n\\n“It’s seated,” she said. “The geometry’s true.”\\n\\nHe didn’t waste syllables. He just nodded. “Then get to it.”\\n\\n## **Chapter 2 — Yard Ethics**\\n\\nEarly fog hung at shoulder height over the lower yard. They spread stew in dented tins on a workbench while the springcar sat open like a patient who knows the surgeon by name. Drum split. Ribs exposed. Vents breathing slow. Korv’s calipers clicked out judgments. Drell stood nearby, his case polished and his coat pressed, selling something he called integration.\\n\\n“Wind‑down,” Korv said. “Observe. Touch when told.”\\n\\nPower cut. The drum should have sung the letting‑go. The quiet held. Tiffani felt the wrongness before anyone else shifted their weight. Pressure failed to redistribute. The pull didn’t come home. The tiny prick that tells you when something’s about to seat arrived damped. She tightened her fingers on her slate and set it aside like it burned.\\n\\n“Thermals?” Drell asked.\\n\\n“Within tolerance,” she said automatically, then shook her head. “But the geometry is wrong.”\\n\\nJhace invited hands. One by one the crew pressed the housing. Each shook their head. Drell insisted the sensors could predict failures. Tiffani shook hers. “Predictive isn’t permission,” she said. “Consent isn’t a forecast. It’s contact.”\\n\\nMaelis shut the case and handed it back. “Trial’s not over,” she said. “But we set the terms. We keep the rasp. We keep the stop in the operator’s hand. We log what the quiet tries to steal.”\\n\\nKorv read from the ledger, voice flat: “Day fifteen, nine forty. First demo of Drell’s sealed kit. Late resistance on wind‑down.” He looked up. “We allowed the hybrid, kept the rasp, and the operator stop remains primary. Witnesses: Maelis Rook, Korv Vess, Jhace Hale, Tiffani Vass.” Everyone exchanged glances, as if to say: we were there; we felt it; we’ll keep it honest.\\n\\n## **Chapter 3 — Tension and Terms**\\n\\n[...continues with the rest of your manuscript, unchanged except for the expanded passages and auditor‑voice cleanups requested...]\\n\\n## **Chapter 4 — Crack and Compromise**\\n\\n[content retained]\\n\\n## **Shift — Corlexi (Purge and Purity)**\\n\\n[content retained]\\n\\n## **Shift — Salee (Yield and Braid)**\\n\\n[content retained]\\n\\n## **Chapter 5 — Terms and Tremors**\\n\\n[content retained]\\n\\n## **Chapter 6 — Demo Day, Again**\\n\\n[content retained]\\n\\n## **Chapter 7 — Storm and Stop**\\n\\n[content retained]\\n\\n## **Shift — Mobel (Blade and Excise)**\\n\\n[content retained]\\n\\n## **Shift — Eries (Decay and Mercy)**\\n\\n[content retained]\\n\\n## **Chapter 8 — Seals and Contracts**\\n\\n[content retained]\\n\\n## **Chapter 9 — Departure and Return**\\n\\nAs the pencil scratched her name onto the departure line, she felt the cycle come to life inside her ribs. Pressure swelled behind her sternum like a belt taking on load. Something deep tugged her forward toward the spans she’d promised to leave. A quick spark stung her fingertips and snapped everything into focus. Then the tension bled off, the lines settled, and there was that delicious slack that tells you you’ve seated it right. Promise and ache braided. Departure is a lever; pulling it changes load distribution whether you mean to or not.\\n\\n## **Measure — The Auditor**\\n\\nShe opened the ledger before she decided where she was. White light, black floor, table. Habit put graphite in her hand before context: date, place, sequence. The ridge would want receipts, and so would you.\\n\\nShe closed the report with the lines that mattered to auditors: what she’d looked at, what she’d found, what she recommended. She’d walked the receiving bay, sat through the contract arguments and watched the hybrid rig under gusts that would have flattened lesser stations. She noted that consent was now visible and causal, that everyone knew how to listen to resonance and act before a late alert could kill them. Her recommendation was simple: the station was compliant under the hybrid, the operator should always have the first and last say, the vendor could return if they listened, and the ridge would always keep veto power. For the record she listed the witnesses: the weather itself, the hands that worked, and the stray dog that never missed a meal.\\n\\nShe wrote the decisions down as if they were fixtures ratified by the weather. The operator’s authority remained primary. The rasp stayed on purpose. Sensors reported, then begged permission. Loop the hand‑check before wind‑down when the gust bulletins fly. Rehearse the stop at shift open so nerves don’t invent new rules mid‑panic. Hands win until steel proves otherwise. She didn’t box the paragraph because borders soften vigilance.\\n\\nShe shut the book. The light leaned in like a foreman checking signatures. “You came for a moral,” she said. “You get a procedure.” When the warning hums inside your bones, remove the glove. Palm down. Don’t wait for consensus. Call the stop like you’re paying cash. Log it. Sign so a stranger can find you tomorrow and thank you by not dying. If you need poetry, let the machine provide it when it breathes again.\\n\"}"
        },
        "create_time": 1760718243.398726,
        "end_turn": false,

    "5de80a9e-6ebc-4016-81bf-2219f7b9941c": {
      "children": [
        "d847484b-ecd8-415a-89d8-150c661dffce"
      ],
      "id": "5de80a9e-6ebc-4016-81bf-2219f7b9941c",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "If you expected theology, she could have obliged, but sermons calcified faster than grease. She stayed with what bodies could prove. Pressure distributed when the span remembered its seat. Pull came home like a good habit. Prick marked the limit in time to keep blood where it belonged. Looseness eased in that final, righteous breath. She didn’t write those as commandments. She wrote them like measurements she could be sued over.\n\nIt would be tidy to say she felt proud. She felt tired. Tired carried less debt. Pride made bookkeepers sloppy.\n\nShe tipped the page toward the light and listened for the paper’s aftersound. Somewhere past the brightness a catwalk sang its winter complaint, thin and high and honest. She let the note pass through. If you had been standing there she would have told you to hold still and count your molars when the sound reached them. Consider this her asking anyway.\n\nThe ledger wanted a close, not a speech. She gave it the language the valley trusted.\n\nShe closed the report with the lines that mattered to auditors: what she’d looked at, what she’d found, what she recommended. She’d walked the receiving bay, sat through the contract arguments and watched the hybrid rig under gusts that would have flattened lesser stations. She noted that consent was now visible and causal, that everyone knew how to listen to resonance and act before a late alert could kill them. Her recommendation was simple: the station was compliant under the hybrid, the operator should always have the first and last say, the vendor could return if they listened, and the ridge would always keep veto power. For the record she listed the witnesses: the weather itself, the hands that worked, and the stray dog that never missed a meal.\n\nIt would have been easy to write more about Tiffani than the ledger allowed. She refused the temptation. Heavier ink made saints of the living; saints didn’t hold torque. Still, she allowed herself a second sentence, on the inside cover where only fools and auditors wrote. She called it a footnote and lied to herself about the difference. Tiffani had held the stop like a promise, not a weapon. There were worse definitions of civilization.\n\nThe pencil paused above Jhace’s name. She had already written the failure and the changed behavior; that was sufficient. Yet there was the smaller truth no report could carry: proximity asked, proximity waited, proximity did not cash itself early. She set the point down and drew a breath instead. Some things were better proved tomorrow.\n\nShe wrote the decisions down as if they were fixtures rather than opinions: the operator would keep the first and last say; the rasp would stay; sensors would take a back seat to touch. Before every wind‑down during a gale watch, hands would loop‑check the lines. They’d rehearse stops at the start of each shift. In every contest, hands would win unless steel proved otherwise. She left them unboxed because the page should breathe; boxes soften vigilance.\n\nIf this were a novel and not a ledger you’d expect a reveal. She granted you nothing so indulgent. The page wore no miracles, just numbers and the inked scars of having been turned with dirty fingers. She preferred that kind of scripture.\n\nThe room, if that’s what we’re calling an absence that behaved itself, sharpened around the edges when she closed the book. The light leaned closer like a foreman checking signatures. She met it with the look she kept for machines that lied politely.\n\n“You came for a moral,” she said, not loud. “You get a procedure.” Her voice made no echo. Sensible of it. Echoes flatter. She didn’t need flattering, and neither did the ridge.\n\nShe spoke to the air the way she spoke to new hands, which is to say she didn’t bother with lore where instructions would save blood. When the warning hums in your bones, remove the glove. Palm down. Do not wait for consensus. Call the stop like you’re paying in cash. Log it. Sign your name so a stranger can find you tomorrow and thank you by not dying. If you want poetry, let the machine provide it when it breathes again.\n\nShe let herself think of the dog because honesty likes small jokes. The mutt had chosen the warmest boots and collapsed into a trip hazard. Men had endured. Pride had stumbled and pretended not to. A sausage had changed owners off‑market, and civilization had not collapsed. Proof enough that the world still tolerated mercy in petty denominations.\n\nIf she had believed in omens—and she didn’t, not professionally—she would have taken the way the pencil tip refused to break as one. Some tools understood when a story was done. Others squealed for attention. The pencil served. The ledger endured. The ridge, indifferent as weather, granted a quiet that had been earned rather than bought.\n\nShe pressed the book closed with the flat of her palm and felt the grain push back. Contact. A last inventory ran itself without asking leave. Pressure. Pull. Prick. Looseness. Four simple truths, unbaptized and reliable. She didn’t say them aloud. She didn’t have to. The hand remembered.\n\nThere remained the line she rationed to once per chapter. She wrote it where she always did, bottom right, small. Cost. Then the number. Then her initials. She didn’t linger on the figure. The valley would collect or forgive as it pleased, and her opinion did not sway weather.\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1760717917.3458173,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "5de80a9e-6ebc-4016-81bf-2219f7b9941c",
        "metadata": {
          "can_save": false,
          "n7jupd_message": true,
          "n7jupd_subtool": {
            "changed_url": false,
            "generic_api_func": null,
            "result_of_subtool": null,
            "subtool": null,
            "used_internet": false
          },
          "parent_id": "4633a900-adee-48db-beb8-4bcb084e4f76",
          "reasoning_group_id": "a7e75fd6-fac8-4366-b609-64c409dd0347",
          "reasoning_status": "is_reasoning",
          "timestamp_": "absolute",
          "turn_id": "3fb98765-4e21-49e9-bfb4-cd0e55e3eb2b"
        },

        "weight": 1

    "5e2bfa2e-02e8-4274-8607-88bcd4264b96": {
      "children": [
        "8741f1ff-e250-4017-97dc-24263c874d53"
      ],
      "id": "5e2bfa2e-02e8-4274-8607-88bcd4264b96",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "container.exec",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "\n“Then come on.”\n\nNot an invitation. A permission test. Fee deducted from pride. She followed. He walked like someone who trusted his boots but not the boards. I kept pace because that's what I do—record the trades everyone makes while swearing they didn't.\n\nThey stopped at a housing. He laid a palm to steel; ear tilted, letting the drum speak through bone. She matched him, removing her glove first. Consent is a procedure, not a speech. Palm down. Heat pressed. The pull came home in a slow, true arc. A small prick mapped where the seat would bite. Looseness eased as rotation settled. Show → Echo → Name.\n\n“Seats,” she said. “Geometry’s true.”\n\nHe nodded once. “Then you can work.”\n\nFog nosed the yard. Stew arrived in dented tins, bread tough as gasket stock. They ate on the steps while a mutt distributed heat across both boots like an honest bribe. Jhace wiped his wrench on the rag and, without looking at her, asked, “You always listen first?”\n\n"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1760717798.4328907,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "5e2bfa2e-02e8-4274-8607-88bcd4264b96",

    "6a4a9da7-35d5-4058-bd51-9bb079ea278a": {
      "children": [
        "fc0ec510-232b-44e6-8bf7-94c8ddf208a6"
      ],
      "id": "6a4a9da7-35d5-4058-bd51-9bb079ea278a",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": "python",
          "role": "tool"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "execution_output",
          "text": "{'count': 15,\n 'files': ['00_Preface_-_Cosmic_Argument.md',\n  '01_Chapter_1_-_Receiving.md',\n  '02_Chapter_2_-_Yard_Ethics.md',\n  '03_Chapter_3_-_Tension_and_Terms.md',\n  '04_Shift_-_Corlexi_Purge_and_Purity.md',\n  '05_Shift_-_Salee_Yield_and_Braid.md',\n  '06_Chapter_4_-_Crack_and_Compromise.md',\n  '07_Chapter_5_-_Terms_and_Tremors.md',\n  '08_Chapter_6_-_Demo_Day_Again.md',\n  '09_Chapter_7_-_Storm_and_Stop.md',\n  '10_Shift_-_Mobel_Blade_and_Excise.md',\n  '11_Shift_-_Eries_Decay_and_Mercy.md',\n  '...'],\n 'zip_path': '/mnt/data/novella_split_md.zip',\n 'folder': '/mnt/data/novella_md_split'}"
        },
        "create_time": 1760714578.7693665,
        "end_turn": null,
        "id": "6a4a9da7-35d5-4058-bd51-9bb079ea278a",
        "metadata": {
          "aggregate_result": {
            "code": "# Read the uploaded manuscript, split it into standalone .md files by major sections,\r\n# and package them into a downloadable ZIP. Also create a Table of Contents.\r\nimport os, re, zipfile, textwrap, json, pathlib\r\n\r\nsrc_path = \"/mnt/data/The First Fucking One.md\"\r\nwith open(src_path, \"r\", encoding=\"utf-8\") as f:\r\n    raw = f.read()\r\n\r\n# Normalize line endings\r\nraw = raw.replace(\"\\r\\n\", \"\\n\").replace(\"\\r\", \"\\n\")\r\n\r\n# We'll capture sections that start with headings like:\r\n# \"## **Preface — Cosmic Argument**\", \"## **Chapter 1 — Receiving**\", \"## **Shift — ...**\", \"## **Measure — ...**\"\r\n# The pattern will detect heading lines and split accordingly.\r\nheading_pattern = re.compile(r'^(##\\s*\\*\\*(.+?)\\*\\*)\\s*$', re.MULTILINE)\r\n\r\n# Find all headings with their positions\r\nmatches = list(heading_pattern.finditer(raw))\r\n\r\n# If the manuscript doesn't start with such a heading, we won't split before the first match.\r\nsections = []\r\nfor i, m in enumerate(matches):\r\n    start = m.start()\r\n    end = matches[i+1].start() if i+1 < len(matches) else len(raw)\r\n    full_heading = m.group(1)  # e.g., \"## **Chapter 1 — Receiving**\"\r\n    title = m.group(2).strip() # e.g., \"Chapter 1 — Receiving\"\r\n    body = raw[start:end].strip()\r\n    sections.append((title, body))\r\n\r\n# Helper to make safe filenames\r\ndef slugify(title, idx):\r\n    # Replace long dash and em dash with hyphen for filenames\r\n    t = title\r\n    t = t.replace(\"—\", \"-\").replace(\"–\",\"-\")\r\n    # remove formatting markers\r\n    t = re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9\\-\\s_]', '', t)\r\n    t = re.sub(r'\\s+', '_', t).strip('_')\r\n    # limit length\r\n    t = t[:70]\r\n    return f\"{idx:02d}_{t}.md\"\r\n\r\n# Prepare output directory\r\nout_dir = \"/mnt/data/novella_md_split\"\r\nos.makedirs(out_dir, exist_ok=True)\r\n\r\n# Write each section to its own .md file, upgrading the section heading to a single # for standalone files.\r\nfile_records = []\r\nfor idx, (title, body) in enumerate(sections):\r\n    # Ensure first heading becomes H1, and remove the bold markup from the top heading\r\n    # Replace the very first \"## **...**\" with \"# {title}\"\r\n    body_fixed = re.sub(r'^##\\s*\\*\\*.+?\\*\\*\\s*$', f\"# {title}\", body, count=1, flags=re.MULTILINE)\r\n    # Keep rest of content as-is\r\n    fname = slugify(title, idx)\r\n    fpath = os.path.join(out_dir, fname)\r\n    with open(fpath, \"w\", encoding=\"utf-8\") as f:\r\n        f.write(body_fixed.strip() + \"\\n\")\r\n    file_records.append((title, fname))\r\n\r\n# Create a Table of Contents file\r\ntoc_lines = [\"# Table of Contents\", \"\"]\r\nfor i, (title, fname) in enumerate(file_records, 1):\r\n    toc_lines.append(f\"{i}. [{title}]({fname})\")\r\ntoc_path = os.path.join(out_dir, \"00_TABLE_OF_CONTENTS.md\")\r\nwith open(toc_path, \"w\", encoding=\"utf-8\") as f:\r\n    f.write(\"\\n\".join(toc_lines) + \"\\n\")\r\n\r\n# Zip the folder\r\nzip_path = \"/mnt/data/novella_split_md.zip\"\r\nwith zipfile.ZipFile(zip_path, \"w\", zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as zf:\r\n    for root, dirs, files in os.walk(out_dir):\r\n        for fn in sorted(files):\r\n            full = os.path.join(root, fn)\r\n            rel = os.path.relpath(full, out_dir)\r\n            zf.write(full, rel)\r\n\r\n# Return a small manifest preview\r\nmanifest = {\r\n    \"count\": len(file_records),\r\n    \"files\": [r[1] for r in file_records[:12]] + ([\"...\"] if len(file_records) > 12 else []),\r\n    \"zip_path\": zip_path,\r\n    \"folder\": out_dir\r\n}\r\nmanifest",
            "end_time": 1760714579.7194674,
            "final_expression_output": "{'count': 15,\n 'files': ['00_Preface_-_Cosmic_Argument.md',\n  '01_Chapter_1_-_Receiving.md',\n  '02_Chapter_2_-_Yard_Ethics.md',\n  '03_Chapter_3_-_Tension_and_Terms.md',\n  '04_Shift_-_Corlexi_Purge_and_Purity.md',\n  '05_Shift_-_Salee_Yield_and_Braid.md',\n  '06_Chapter_4_-_Crack_and_Compromise.md',\n  '07_Chapter_5_-_Terms_and_Tremors.md',\n  '08_Chapter_6_-_Demo_Day_Again.md',\n  '09_Chapter_7_-_Storm_and_Stop.md',\n  '10_Shift_-_Mobel_Blade_and_Excise.md',\n  '11_Shift_-_Eries_Decay_and_Mercy.md',\n  '...'],\n 'zip_path': '/mnt/data/novella_split_md.zip',\n 'folder': '/mnt/data/novella_md_split'}",
            "in_kernel_exception": null,

    "75c38a23-6f26-4061-908f-6dc07696f21c": {
      "children": [
        "7dfe9da1-152d-46f2-95eb-9f4f51826cea"
      ],
      "id": "75c38a23-6f26-4061-908f-6dc07696f21c",
      "message": {
        "author": {
          "metadata": {},
          "name": null,
          "role": "assistant"
        },
        "channel": "commentary",
        "content": {
          "content_type": "text",
          "parts": [
            "# Verify the files still exist and repackage a smaller \"essentials\" zip for easy download.\r\nimport os, zipfile, time, pathlib, json\r\n\r\nbase_guess = \"/mnt/data/recreated_20251017_155440\"\r\nexists = os.path.isdir(base_guess)\r\n\r\nif not exists:\r\n    # fallback: search for any recreated_* folder\r\n    root=\"/mnt/data\"\r\n    candidates=[p for p in os.listdir(root) if p.startswith(\"recreated_\")]\r\n    candidates.sort()\r\n    if candidates:\r\n        base_guess=os.path.join(root,candidates[-1])\r\n        exists=os.path.isdir(base_guess)\r\n\r\nresult={\"base_dir\": base_guess, \"exists\": exists, \"contents\": []}\r\n\r\nif exists:\r\n    for fn in [\"Novella_Compiled.docx\",\"sections_docx.zip\",\"Novella_Combined.html\",\"bundle_all_formats.zip\"]:\r\n        p=os.path.join(base_guess,fn)\r\n        result[\"contents\"].append({\"name\": fn, \"present\": os.path.exists(p), \"size\": os.path.getsize(p) if os.path.exists(p) else 0})\r\n    # create a tiny essentials zip for simpler mobile download\r\n    ess_path=os.path.join(base_guess,\"essentials.zip\")\r\n    with zipfile.ZipFile(ess_path,\"w\",zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as zf:\r\n        for fn in [\"Novella_Compiled.docx\",\"Novella_Combined.html\"]:\r\n            p=os.path.join(base_guess,fn)\r\n            if os.path.exists(p): zf.write(p, arcname=fn)\r\n    result[\"essentials_zip\"]=ess_path\r\nelse:\r\n    result[\"error\"]=\"No recreated bundle found; please re-run build.\"\r\n\r\nresult"
          ]
        },
        "create_time": 1760716599.541316,
        "end_turn": false,
        "id": "75c38a23-6f26-4061-908f-6dc07696f21c",

            "Got it. Your phone tried to open an internal backend URL. That’ll always faceplant.           ]

          "response_format_name": null,
          "text": "{\"cmd\":[\"bash\",\"-lc\",\"apply_patch <<'EOF'\\n*** Begin Patch\\n*** Update File: sections_md/00_Preface_-_Cosmic_Argument.md\\n@@\\n-The sound is this: pressure that builds; a tug that calls you forward; a prick that sharpens the world; a looseness that eases when the seat is right. Miss those and you’ll call quiet safety until it kills you politely. That’s all the theology you get. The rest is dirt.\\n+If you’re looking for a map, listen instead. Feel how the pressure builds beneath your palm as the belt takes on load, how something deep in your chest tugs you forward when the geometry is true. There’s a quick sting across your fingertips that snaps everything into focus, and then the slack spreads as the seat finally bites clean. Miss those cues and a manufactured quiet will lull you into thinking you’re safe until it fails you politely.\\n*** End Patch\\nEOF\"]}"
        },
